back_translation: As for his own palace, Solomon took thirteen years to finish building it completely.
He built the House of the Forest of Lebanon, a hundred cubits long, fifty cubits wide, and thirty cubits high, with four rows of cedar pillars supporting cedar beams.
It was roofed with cedar above the beams that rested on the pillars—forty-five beams, fifteen per row.
There were three rows of windows facing one another in three tiers.
All the doorways and frames were rectangular, with openings facing one another in three tiers.
He made the colonnade fifty cubits long and thirty cubits wide, with a portico in front of it, and pillars and a canopy in front of the portico.
He built the throne hall, the Hall of Justice, where he would render judgment; it was paneled with cedar from floor to ceiling.
His own palace where he would live, in another court set further back, was of similar construction. He also made a palace like this hall for Pharaoh's daughter, whom he had married.
All these were of costly stones, cut to size, trimmed with saws inside and out, from the foundation to the eaves, and from the outside to the great courtyard.
The foundations were of large, costly stones—some ten cubits, some eight cubits.
Above were costly stones cut to size, and cedar beams.
The great courtyard was surrounded by three rows of dressed stone and a row of trimmed cedar beams, like the inner courtyard of the house of the LORD and the portico of the house.
King Solomon sent and brought Huram from Tyre.

He was the son of a widow from the tribe of Naphtali, and his father was a man of Tyre, a craftsman in bronze. Huram was full of skill, understanding, and knowledge for every kind of bronze work. He came to King Solomon and did all his work.
He cast two pillars of bronze, each eighteen cubits high and twelve cubits in circumference.
He made two capitals of cast bronze to set on the tops of the pillars, each capital five cubits high.
For the capitals on top of the pillars he made networks of lattice and wreaths of chainwork, seven on each side for each capital.
He made the pillars, and two rows of pomegranates around each network to cover the capitals on top of the pillars; so he did for both capitals.
The capitals on top of the pillars in the portico were shaped like lilies, four cubits high.
On the capitals of both pillars, above the rounded projection next to the network, were two hundred pomegranates in rows encircling each capital.
He set up the pillars at the portico of the temple; the pillar on the right (south) he named Jachin, and the pillar on the left (north) he named Boaz.
On the tops of the pillars was lily-work. So the work of the pillars was finished.
He cast the Sea of metal, circular in shape, ten cubits from rim to rim, five cubits high, and thirty cubits in circumference.
Below its rim were ornamental buds encircling it, ten per cubit all around the Sea, cast in two rows together with the Sea in one casting.
The Sea stood on twelve oxen, three facing north, three facing west, three facing south, and three facing east; the Sea rested on them, with all their hindquarters toward the center.
It was a handbreadth thick, and its rim was made like the brim of a cup, like a lily blossom. It held two thousand baths.
He made ten movable stands of bronze, each four cubits long, four cubits wide, and three cubits high.
This was the design of the stands: they had panels set in frames.
On the panels between the frames were lions, oxen, and cherubim; on the frames was a pedestal above, and below the lions and oxen were hanging wreaths.
Each stand had four bronze wheels with bronze axles, and a basin resting on four supports, with wreaths at each side.

Its opening inside the crown at the top was one cubit deep; the opening was round, like the design of a pedestal, a cubit and a half wide; and around its opening were engravings, but the panels were square, not round.
The four wheels were under the panels, and the axles of the wheels were attached to the stand; each wheel was a cubit and a half in diameter.
The wheels were made like chariot wheels; their axles, rims, spokes, and hubs were all cast metal.
Each stand had four handles, one at each corner, projecting from the stand.
At the top of the stand was a circular band half a cubit high; the supports and panels were cast as one with the top of the stand.
He engraved cherubim, lions, and palm trees on the surfaces of the supports and panels, wherever there was space, with wreaths all around.
In this way he made the ten stands, all with one casting, one measure, and one shape.
He made ten bronze basins, each holding forty baths and measuring four cubits, one basin for each of the ten stands.
He set five stands on the south side of the house and five on the north, and he set the Sea on the south side, at the southeast corner of the house.
Huram also made the pots, the shovels, and the sprinkling bowls. So Huram finished all the work he did for King Solomon for the house of the LORD:
the two pillars; the two bowl-shaped capitals on top of the pillars; the two networks covering the two bowl-shaped capitals on top of the pillars;
the four hundred pomegranates for the two networks (two rows of pomegranates for each network, covering the two bowl-shaped capitals on top of the pillars);
the ten stands; the ten basins on the stands;
the one Sea; the twelve oxen under the Sea;
the pots, the shovels, and the sprinkling bowls. All these articles that Huram made for King Solomon for the house of the LORD were of burnished bronze.
In the plain of the Jordan the king cast them, in clay molds, between Succoth and Zarethan.
Solomon left all these articles unweighed because there were so many; the weight of the bronze could not be determined.
Solomon also made all the furnishings for the house of the LORD: the golden altar; the golden table on which was the Bread of the Presence;
the lampstands of pure gold, five on the right and five on the left in front of the inner sanctuary; the gold flowers, lamps, and tongs;

the basins, wick trimmers, sprinkling bowls, ladles, and firepans of pure gold; and the gold hinges for the doors of the inner room, the Most Holy Place, and for the doors of the main hall of the temple.

So all the work that King Solomon did for the house of the LORD was finished. Then Solomon brought in what David his father had dedicated—the silver, the gold, and the articles—and he placed them in the treasuries of the house of the LORD.
